era_context: The early 16th century continues with the Renaissance in Europe, the Ottoman Empire consolidating power, the Ming Dynasty in its mature phase, and European colonial expansion accelerating. The Reformation is still four years away. Indigenous American civilisations remain independent of European control.

events.title: Election of Pope Leo X (Giovanni de' Medici)
events.description: Giovanni de' Medici was elected Pope on 11 March 1513, taking the name Leo X. At age 37, he became one of the youngest popes in centuries and the first Medici pope. Leo X was known for his patronage of Renaissance art and culture, his diplomatic skills, and his involvement in Italian politics. His election marked a significant moment in the history of the papacy, as the Medici family gained direct control of the papal office. Leo X's reign would be marked by lavish spending on art and architecture, including the continuation of St. Peter's Basilica, which contributed to the financial pressures that led to the sale of indulgences and provoked Martin Luther's criticism in 1517.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts and later ecclesiastical histories

events.certainty_note: Leo X's election on 11 March 1513 is well-documented in papal records.

events.title: Battle of Flodden Field; Scottish defeat by English forces
events.description: King James IV of Scotland was defeated and killed at the Battle of Flodden Field on 9 September 1513 by English forces under the Earl of Surrey. The battle was one of the largest and bloodiest battles fought between England and Scotland, with estimates of 10,000–20,000 Scottish casualties. The Scottish defeat was a major blow to Scottish power and prestige, and it resulted in the death of the Scottish king and much of the Scottish nobility. The battle had significant consequences for Scottish politics, as it led to a regency for the young James V and weakened Scotland's ability to resist English expansion. The battle is remembered in Scottish history as a national tragedy.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Flodden Field on 9 September 1513 is extensively documented in English and Scottish sources.
cross_references: 1542-001 (Battle of Solway Moss and James V's death)

events.title: Vasco Núñez de Balboa discovers the Pacific Ocean
events.description: Spanish explorer Vasco Núñez de Balboa, leading an expedition from the Caribbean coast of present-day Panama, crossed the Isthmus of Panama and reached the Pacific Ocean on 25 September 1513. Balboa named the ocean the 'Mar del Sur' (South Sea), and his discovery provided crucial geographical knowledge about the Americas. The expedition involved indigenous guides and porters, and it resulted in the death of many indigenous people from disease and violence. Balboa's discovery demonstrated that a large ocean lay west of the Caribbean and suggested the possibility of a westward passage to Asia. The discovery had significant implications for European understanding of global geography and for future exploration and colonisation efforts.

sources.name: Peter Martyr d'Anghiera, De Orbe Novo
Spanish royal records and later compilations

events.certainty_note: Balboa's discovery of the Pacific Ocean on 25 September 1513 is well-documented in contemporary sources, particularly Peter Martyr's account.

events.title: Portuguese naval victory at Diu; consolidation of Indian Ocean control
events.description: Portuguese naval forces, under the command of Afonso de Albuquerque, defeated an Ottoman and Arab fleet at the Battle of Diu in 1513. The victory consolidated Portuguese naval dominance in the Indian Ocean and secured Portuguese control of key trade routes. The battle involved the use of Portuguese gunpowder technology and naval tactics against traditional Arab and Ottoman naval forces. The Portuguese victory at Diu was a significant moment in the establishment of European naval dominance in the Indian Ocean and contributed to the disruption of traditional Arab and Indian merchant networks. The battle demonstrated the effectiveness of European naval technology and tactics against non-European forces.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Governor of India)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and naval logs
Later Portuguese historiography (Gaspar Correia, Diogo do Couto)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Diu in 1513 is documented in Portuguese records, though some sources date it to 1509 or conflate it with other naval battles.

events.title: Ming Dynasty's Zhengde Emperor's reign; eunuch influence and court politics
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor of the Ming Dynasty continued his reign in 1513, characterised by the significant influence of eunuchs in court politics, particularly the eunuch Liu Jin. The Zhengde Emperor was known for his personal extravagance, his interest in military affairs, and his willingness to delegate authority to trusted eunuchs. This period saw the rise of eunuch power in the Ming court, which would have long-term consequences for Ming governance. The Zhengde Emperor's reign was marked by military campaigns against Mongol incursions, cultural patronage, and the continuation of Ming administrative systems. His reliance on eunuchs was controversial and contributed to factional conflicts within the court.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Ming court records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign and eunuch influence are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Selim I becomes Ottoman Sultan; beginning of Ottoman expansion
events.description: Selim I became Ottoman Sultan in 1512 (not 1513, but his consolidation of power and major military campaigns occurred in 1513). Selim I, known as 'Selim the Grim,' was one of the most significant Ottoman sultans, known for his military campaigns and his expansion of Ottoman territory. In 1513, Selim I was consolidating his power and preparing for major military campaigns against the Safavid Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate. Selim I's reign would be marked by significant territorial expansion, the conquest of Egypt and the Levant, and the establishment of Ottoman dominance in the Mediterranean and the Near East. His reign represented a turning point in Ottoman history, as the empire shifted from a regional power to a major world power.
events.certainty_note: Selim I's ascension to the Ottoman throne occurred in 1512, but his consolidation of power and major military campaigns in 1513 are well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Flodden Field is sometimes presented as a decisive English victory that permanently shifted the balance of power between England and Scotland, but modern scholarship notes that Scotland remained a significant power and that English expansion was gradual rather than sudden. Balboa's discovery of the Pacific Ocean is sometimes presented as a singular achievement, but modern scholarship emphasises the role of indigenous guides and the fact that indigenous peoples had long known of the ocean's existence. The Battle of Diu is sometimes dated to 1509 or conflated with other naval battles; sources vary on the exact date and significance. Selim I's reign is sometimes presented as a sudden transformation of Ottoman power, but modern scholarship emphasises the gradual nature of Ottoman expansion and the importance of earlier Ottoman sultans.
historiographic_note: 1513 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for Spain, England, Portugal, and the Papacy. Ottoman sources are substantial but often written from the Ottoman perspective. Chinese sources are rich but primarily available in later compilations. Indigenous American perspectives are entirely absent from contemporary written records; European accounts are the only surviving documentation. The year reflects the global dominance of European documentation in surviving historical records.

graph_edges.note: Leo X's election led to his extensive patronage of Renaissance art
Leo X's spending on art and indulgences contributed to financial pressures that provoked Reformation
Battle of Flodden Field weakened Scotland and contributed to later English-Scottish conflicts
Balboa's discovery of Pacific Ocean contributed to European understanding of global geography
Portuguese naval operations in Indian Ocean were ongoing
Zhengde Emperor's reign ended with his death in 1516
Selim I's consolidation of power led to Ottoman conquest of Egypt
